figures.caption: Fig. 3: Example relative soil moisture (𝑠), sensible heat (𝐻), CAPE, and boundary layer height evolution for a full dry-down beginning at 𝑠 = 1. The parameter values are the same as in Figure 2, with a 𝜓50,stem = -4 MPa.

Fig. 6: Grouped plot for each land cover type, showing time series of soil moisture, corresponding histograms, and scatter plot of averaged frequency over 50 days (?̄?) versus initial soil moisture (𝑠𝑖).

Fig. 7: Latitude-dependent grouped plot for each landcover type, showing time series of soil moisture, corresponding histograms, and scatter plot of averaged frequency over 50 days (?̄?) versus initial soil moisture (𝑠𝑖). Importantly, grass/cropland at 37◦ has atmospheric conditions that do not produce crossing times; the soil moisture at crossing histogram, in this case, is the histogram of 𝑠.

Fig. 2: Diurnal evolution of stomatal conductance, LCL height (red lines), ABL height (blue lines), and CAPE throughout the day for three initial relative soil moisture conditions: 0.5, 0.4, and 0.3, from left to right. The atmospheric parameters are taken from Yin et al. (2015) based on early morning measurements at the Central Facility, Southern Great Plains. The plant parameters (i.e., 𝐿𝐴𝐼, 𝑍𝑟 , and 𝑔𝑎) are taken from the Forest tab in Table 2, given a sandy loam soil. The black stars represent the crossing time of the LCL and ABL.

Fig. 5: CAPE and convective event precipitation as a function of surface temperature and specific humidity values shown by the contour plot, given that the ABL has just reached the LCL, given an average storm duration time of 20 minutes, and precipitation efficiency, 𝜀 = 𝜀BL, of 0.1.
Table 1: Key variables and symbols used in the model.

Fig. 1: Schematic showing the temperature profiles within the atmospheric boundary layer and in the free atmosphere, including the moist temperature profile as in Eq. (A8). The shaded yellow and red areas between the two temperature profiles represent CIN and CAPE, respectively. It is assumed that a parcel follows a dry adiabatic process until it reaches the LCL.

Fig. 4: Panel (a) represents CAPE at crossing time vs. day of dry-down for different soil types. Panel (b) illustrates the same dry-down for four different 𝜓50,stem values, given the same parameters as Figure 3, other than the modification of 𝜓50,stem and soil type. Panels (c) and (d) represent CAPE at crossing vs. soil moisture, (𝑠).
